TextENGNew: Beneath a blazing sun that called midsummer to mind, a group of boys chased the white ball with single-minded abandon.
changeReason: New translation adds specific details from the Japanese: midsummer imagery, 'chased the white ball with single-minded abandon' versus generic 'playing baseball'.

TextENGNew: The chorus of cicadas, which only made the heat feel more oppressive, was to those children nothing more than another voice cheering them on.
Of course, they ran through the fundamentals properly and did their conditioning drills.
changeReason: New translation distinguishes two activities (fundamentals and conditioning drills) matching the Japanese, while the original collapsed them into one.

TextENGNew: But if truth be told, the children weren't fond of those regimented exercises—what they really lived for was the free scrimmage at the end of practice.
The school in Hinamizawa was originally forestry bureau property, and the children merely borrowed its grounds.
As a result, the schoolyard was all gravel—hardly suited for vigorous play.
By contrast, the athletic grounds at this school in Okinomiya were properly maintained, and the children grew all the more spirited for it.
Not that the Hinamizawa children particularly cared whether they were playing on gravel or grass.
The other coach was watching over the game, so I decided to use that time to go buy juice for the kids.
changeReason: New specifies 'juice' and 'kids' rather than generic 'drinks' and 'team', and adds 'use that time' matching the Japanese temporal context.

TextENGNew: "Coach, where are you going?"

"I thought I'd go pick up some juice for everyone.
If you're willing, I'd be very grateful for your help, Satoshi-kun."
"Oh—yes, of course.@k@vS20/07/VTK1_satoshi1003. I'll help."
changeReason: Opposite meaning: JPN says あ、はい。お手伝いします (Oh, yes, I'll help) but old says 'Oh no, not at all!' — agreement vs. refusal.

TextENGNew: Satoshi-kun gave a ready answer and fell into step beside me.
When adults pick out juice on their own, the children often complain—having him along was a genuine help.
Together, Satoshi-kun and I drove to the supermarket down the road.

"...Coach.
How is Satoko doing...?"

"Yes.
She's progressing well.
Very slowly, but Satoko-chan's emotional wounds are beginning to heal.
I think we should let her rest a little longer."
It was only natural for Satoshi-kun to worry.
Satoko-chan had been hospitalized for more than two weeks already.
He did not know, of course, that his sister was afflicted with Hinamizawa Syndrome—or that she had reached the precipice of L5, where a complete breakdown could come at any moment.
changeReason: Old says 'almost at L5' but JPN 瀬戸際のL5 means she is at the precipice OF L5 (already there). The distinction in severity level matters for the plot.

TextENGNew: He believed she was simply bedridden from the shock of their parents' accident.

He had been coming every day to cheer her up—
but Satoko-chan's emotional state had grown unstable of late, showing signs dangerous enough that we had been forced to turn away her visitors.
To be honest... Satoko-chan's condition was not good.
The state Satoko-chan had entered—L5—was already beyond anything that could be called ordinary.
The man last year who had reached the same stage was in a condition so intractable that any physician would have thrown up their hands.
changeReason: Original uses 'drug addict in his final stages' metaphor which is absent from the Japanese; new translation correctly conveys 'any physician would have given up', matching the Japanese 'even any doctor would throw up their hands'.

TextENGNew: ...There was absolutely no guarantee that Satoko-chan would not end up the same way, with the slightest provocation.
True, the dissection of that man last year had revealed to us the true nature of Hinamizawa Syndrome and done much to illuminate its mechanisms.
...We had also made precious discoveries that might one day lead to a cure.

But a treatment had yet to be established—and if Satoko-chan were to fall into an unrecoverable state,
...she might face dissection, just as the man last year had...
I could not bring myself to tell Satoshi-kun that his sister stood at the threshold between life and death. All I could do was speak in vague terms and lie to him that she was recovering steadily.
If something were to happen to her—
...I would have told Satoshi-kun an unforgivable lie.
...The faint remnant of conscience still living inside me gave a small, sharp pang.

"...If it wouldn't be too much trouble for your clinic...
maybe it would be better to keep her hospitalized a little longer."
"...What do you mean by that?"
I had assumed he was hoping for her discharge as soon as possible—I was a little surprised.

"No, it's just...
The truth is, Satoko...
...she hasn't really been able to fit in with our aunt's family."

"I had heard that Satoko-chan's relationship with her parents wasn't a good one,
...but now with the aunt and uncle she's been placed with as well...?"
"............Satoko just...doesn't open up to adults very well."
Satoshi-kun had told me something of Satoko-chan's unhappy upbringing.
...Their mother's repeated remarriages had inflicted deep wounds on both children.
Satoshi-kun had been able to get past it—but Satoko-chan had been too young at the time to be expected to do the same.
The trauma had left her with a profound aversion to any guardian who wasn't bound to her by blood.
...Well—anyone would dislike having a guardian they shared no blood with.
It wasn't unique to Satoko-chan.
But most people feel the discomfort inwardly and press it down, hidden.
...For Satoko-chan, no matter how hard she tried, that was simply beyond her reach...

"Our uncle doesn't come home much, and even when he's there he's either asleep or watching TV, so he's not really the problem...
It's our aunt—she's always getting after Satoko."
"...I wonder why Satoko-chan ended up so disliked by her..."
changeReason: Subject reversed: JPN asks why Satoko ended up so disliked (嫌われてしまった, passive), but old asks 'Why doesn't she like her?' — reverses who dislikes whom.

TextENGNew: I could make a reasonable guess.
...Because Satoko-chan's parents had supported the dam, the aunt and uncle had doubtless suffered for it too, simply by bearing the Houjou name.
And on top of that, the parents had gone and died in an accident, foisting two children on them—there would be resentment to spare there, and precious little goodwill.
Even so, if she had managed to keep the peace outwardly, as Satoshi-kun did, things would not have come to a head so easily.
...But Satoko-chan could not be quiet.
Her feelings showed in her every expression and gesture, and it escalated into bitter quarreling.

"...I've tried to explain Satoko's situation to my aunt, but...
She just doesn't seem to understand..."

"Every time you step in to defend Satoko-chan, I think to myself what a model older brother you are.
You may be the only true family she has left in this world, Satoshi-kun."
"...Yes. ...That's how I feel too."
He said it with just the faintest trace of a sigh woven through his words.
He would never say it aloud, but it seemed that Satoshi-kun was exhausted—exhausted from being dragged into the middle of things every time Satoko-chan and their aunt came to blows.
He was a good person and a good brother.
That was precisely why he would always stand and fight for his sister.
...But that was an extraordinarily exhausting way to live.
Satoshi-kun had been living that way since back when their parents were still alive.
And eventually, his mental equilibrium cracked under the weight of it all, and he found his way to my clinic.
I had thought that with the root cause—his parents—now gone, things might have improved...
But things appeared to be even more serious now than they had been then...

"I understand.
If that's the situation, discharging Satoko-chan now might cause her emotional symptoms to relapse."

"...Please don't worry about the hospital fees.
I'll take full responsibility for her care until Satoko-chan has made a true recovery."
...I felt a small, creeping revulsion at my own cunning—the way I dressed my own convenience in the clothing of compassion, pretending to heed his wish.
"Full responsibility for her care"—what hollow words those were.
changeReason: Old's vague 'I'll make sure?' misses JPN's bitter self-reproach: 何が、責任を持ってお預かりなのか means 'what hollow words "full responsibility for her care" were.'

TextENGNew: ...Takano-san was methodically advancing her plans for the girl's dissection even as we spoke...
"...If you could do that... it would help me too, just a little."

"...No, it's nothing. ............Just that if Satoko stayed hospitalized a little longer... I'd be able to breathe a little easier too.
That's all."
"What do you mean by that...?"

"...When Satoko comes back, I know she'll end up fighting with our aunt again.
And then it'll be back to those days of nothing but shouting and quarreling... and I'll be pulled into the middle of it all again."

"...Part of me thinks that the easiest thing—for me—would be for Satoko to just stay hospitalized forever... And when I catch myself thinking that...
...I hate myself for it..."
changeReason: Original says 'hospitalized for a very long time' while new says 'stay hospitalized forever', which better matches the Japanese ずっと (always/forever) and conveys a more extreme sentiment.

TextENGNew: "...Satoshi-kun..."
Satoshi-kun was a good brother who always stood up for his sister.
...But that did not mean the burden weighed nothing on him...

"...To even think something like that... what a terrible brother I am.
And the more I think that, the more I despise myself.
............"
He said this, and gave a single quiet sniff.
His face was filled with anguish—he looked as though he were bearing the full weight of his own guilt...
The very desire to be a model older brother was driving him into a corner.
...Satoko-chan was pitiable, certainly—but in a different way, so was Satoshi-kun.
As someone who knew them both well—as the only person who truly saw them for what they were—I felt keenly that I must be their ally, for no one else would stand beside them...
